**wiki layer design for context management**

The Issue Queue relies on the wiki as the living source of truth, so understanding the progressive disclosure model clarifies why issues feed into Layer 3. Linking to the Progressive Disclosure article helps explain how the system scopes information loading and avoids unnecessary context.

### Models and Providers
**LLM providers powering edge‑discovery evaluations**

The Issue Queue creates durable GitLab issues that the heartbeat later processes. The heartbeat uses the LLM models listed in the Models and Providers article to evaluate whether two wiki pages should be linked.

### Launchpad
**Core agent platform hosting the Issue Queue**

The Issue Queue runs inside the Launchpad Firebase agent, relying on its Cloud Functions for webhook handling and LLM evaluation. Understanding the Launchpad architecture explains why the queue can persist, be inspected, and be cancelled via issue labels.

### Development Guide
**Where the queue code and deployment live**

The Development Guide shows the repository layout, Cloud Functions, and secrets that power the webhook handler and heartbeat which process the issue queue. Understanding this guide helps developers locate and modify the code that creates and claims queue issues.

### Update Handler
**Update Handler files issues for page edits**

The Issue Queue relies on the Update Handler to generate issues whenever a wiki page is edited. These issues feed the queue, enabling persistent and cancellable processing of edge‑discovery work.

### Platform Vision
**Architectural overview of the shared platform**

The Platform Vision article explains the overarching design principles—pulse, dispatch, a single queue, and the handler pattern—that the Issue Queue relies on for its coordination layer. It provides the high‑level context that justifies the Issue Queue’s role as a durable job queue within the broader automation system.

### Executor Contract
**Firestore job schema for Launchpad‑Phoenix execution**

The Executor Contract defines the Firestore job interface that Launchpad uses to dispatch work to the Phoenix executor. Familiarity with this contract is useful for integrating the Issue Queue’s GitLab‑based workflow with other pipeline components.

### GitHub‑GitLab Workflow
**source of truth and AI playground integration**

The Issue Queue relies on the GitHub‑GitLab Workflow to obtain a clean source of truth and a sandbox for AI agents. This workflow explains why issues are mirrored from GitHub and acted upon in GitLab.

[links]` appended to the new page
2. One issue filed per existing wiki page (N-1 issues total)
3. Each issue compares the new page against one existing page

**On `action: "update"`** (not yet implemented)

1. Strip `## Related` section from the page
2. Re-insert `[links]`
3. Close any open pending issues for this page (reset the queue)
4. File N-1 fresh issues — identical to create

**On `action: "delete"`** (not yet implemented)

Remove edges pointing at the deleted page from all other pages. No issues needed — direct wiki edits.

---

## How to Inspect the Queue

In GitLab → Issues → filter by label `wiki-edge`:

- **Open + `status:pending`** — waiting to be processed
- **Open + `status:claimed`** — heartbeat is working on them (or crashed mid-process)
- **Closed** — done

To cancel a pending pair before the heartbeat runs it: close the issue manually.

To check what ran: GitLab Issues (closed) or Firebase Console → Firestore → `agent_actions` → filter by `type: "onWikiEvent"`.

---

## The [links] Placeholder

`[links]` is appended to every new page by `onWikiEvent`. It serves two purposes:

1. **Marks where `## Related` will go** — the heartbeat writes edges at this position
2. **Validation flag** — any page still containing `[links]` after 11+ minutes has unresolved edges (backlog: validation script to surface these)

On a page that's been fully processed, `[links]` is replaced with a `## Related` section. If `[links]` is still present, the queue either hasn't drained yet or something failed.

---

title: Models and Providers
content:
# Models and Providers

Reference page for every LLM provider and model the agent can use. Check here before picking a model for a new ability.

---

## Active Providers

| Provider | Base URL | Auth | Secret Name | SDK |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DeepInfra | `https://api.deepinfra.com/v1/openai` | Bearer token | `DEEPINFRA_API_KEY` | `openai` (OpenAI-compatible) |
| Cerebras | `https://api.cerebras.ai/v1` | Bearer token | `CEREBRAS_API_KEY` | `@cerebras/cerebras_cloud_sdk` |

**Current default:** DeepInfra with `openai/gpt-oss-120b`

---

## DeepInfra

**Docs:** https://deepinfra.com/docs
**Dashboard:** https://deepinfra.com/dash
**Signup:** https://deepinfra.com/signup
**SDK:** `openai` (npm) — standard OpenAI SDK with custom base URL

DeepInfra is a multi-model inference platform. OpenAI-compatible API. No special SDK required — just point the OpenAI client at their endpoint.

### Role in the System

**Primary provider.** Runs the active model for all current abilities. Chosen for low cost and OpenAI SDK compatibility.

Currently powers: `onGitLabPush` (doc correction ability) using `openai/gpt-oss-120b`

### Models

| Model ID | Params | Architecture | Context | Pricing (in/out per 1M) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `openai/gpt-oss-120b` | 117B | MoE | 131,072 | $0.039 / $0.19 | **Active — current default** |
| `openai/gpt-oss-20b` | 21B | MoE (3.6B active) | 131,072 | $0.03 / $0.14 | Available — cheaper fallback |

Other notable models on DeepInfra (not currently used but available):

| Model ID | Pricing (in/out per 1M) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `meta-llama/Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct` | $0.08 / $0.30 | Llama 4, good mid-tier option |
| `deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3.2` | $0.26 / $0.38 | Strong reasoning, larger |
| `deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1-0528` | $0.50 / $2.15 | Deep reasoning, expensive output |

### Capabilities

`openai/gpt-oss-120b` supports:
- Function calling and tool use
- Structured outputs (JSON mode)
- Configurable reasoning depth with full chain-of-thought access
- Streaming
- 131K context window (both input and output)
- Fine-tuning (via DeepInfra platform)

### API Usage

```typescript
import OpenAI from "openai";

const client = new OpenAI({
  baseURL: "https://api.deepinfra.com/v1/openai",
  apiKey: process.env.DEEPINFRA_API_KEY,
});

const completion = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: "openai/gpt-oss-120b",
  messages: [
    { role: "system", content: "System prompt" },
    { role: "user", content: "User input" },
  ],
});
```

### Test Command

```bash
DI_KEY=$(gcloud secrets versions access latest --secret=DEEPINFRA_API_KEY --project=launchpad-ai-orchestrator) && \
curl -s "https://api.deepinfra.com/v1/openai/chat/completions" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $DI_KEY" \
  -d '{"model":"openai/gpt-oss-120b","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Say hello."}],"max_tokens":10}'
```

### List Available Models

```bash
DI_KEY=$(gcloud secrets versions access latest --secret=DEEPINFRA_API_KEY --project=launchpad-ai-orchestrator) && \
curl -s "https://api.deepinfra.com/v1/openai/models" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $DI_KEY" | python3 -m json.tool
```

---

## Cerebras

**Docs:** https://inference-docs.cerebras.ai
**Dashboard:** https://cloud.cerebras.ai
**SDK:** `@cerebras/cerebras_cloud_sdk` (npm)

Cerebras runs inference on their own wafer-scale hardware. Extremely fast — 2000-3000 tokens/second. OpenAI-compatible API.

### Role in the System

**Secondary provider.** Available for future abilities that need Cerebras-specific speed or models not on DeepInfra. Client wrapper exists at `functions/src/lib/cerebras.ts` but is not currently imported by any active ability.

### Models

| Model ID | Params | Speed | Status | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `llama3.1-8b` | 8B | ~2200 tok/s | Production | Fast cheap tasks, simple evaluations |
| `gpt-oss-120b` | 120B | ~3000 tok/s | Production | Balanced quality + speed |
| `qwen-3-235b-a22b-instruct-2507` | 235B | ~1400 tok/s | Preview | Heavy reasoning, complex analysis |
| `zai-glm-4.7` | 355B | ~1000 tok/s | Preview | Untested — largest available |

### Rate Limits (Free Tier)

| Model | Tokens/min |
|---|---|
| `llama3.1-8b` | 60K |
| `gpt-oss-120b` | 64K |
| `qwen-3-235b-a22b-instruct-2507` | 60K |
| `zai-glm-4.7` | 60K |

### Rate Limits (PayGo Tier)

| Model | Tokens/min |
|---|---|
| `llama3.1-8b` | 2M |
| `gpt-oss-120b` | 1M |
| `qwen-3-235b-a22b-instruct-2507` | 250K |
| `zai-glm-4.7` | 500K |

### Pricing Tiers

| Tier | Cost | What You Get |
|---|---|---|
| Free | $0 | All models, baseline rate limits |
| Developer | $10+ | 10x rate limits, pay-as-you-go |
| Enterprise | Custom | Dedicated support, fine-tuning |

### API Usage

```typescript
import Cerebras from "@cerebras/cerebras_cloud_sdk";

const client = new Cerebras({
  apiKey: "csk-...",
  warmTCPConnection: false,
});

const completion = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: "gpt-oss-120b",
  messages: [
    { role: "system", content: "System prompt" },
    { role: "user", content: "User input" },
  ],
});
```

### Test Command

```bash
CEREBRAS_KEY=$(gcloud secrets versions access latest --secret=CEREBRAS_API_KEY --project=launchpad-ai-orchestrator) && \
curl -s https://api.cerebras.ai/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $CEREBRAS_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"gpt-oss-120b","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Say hello."}],"max_tokens":10}'
```

---

## Cost Comparison

For a typical doc review task (~2K input tokens, ~500 output tokens):

| Provider | Model | Input Cost | Output Cost | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DeepInfra | gpt-oss-120b | $0.000078 | $0.000095 | **$0.000173** |
| DeepInfra | gpt-oss-20b | $0.00006 | $0.00007 | **$0.00013** |
| Cerebras (Free) | gpt-oss-120b | $0 | $0 | **$0** |

At current usage levels, cost is negligible on any provider. DeepInfra was chosen for API simplicity (standard OpenAI SDK) and consistent availability without tier limits.

---

## Choosing a Model for a New Ability

```
Is the task simple and repetitive?
  (format validation, simple classification, yes/no gate)
    → openai/gpt-oss-20b via DeepInfra (cheapest)
    → llama3.1-8b via Cerebras (fastest, free tier)

Is the task moderate?
  (diff evaluation, doc correction, code review)
    → openai/gpt-oss-120b via DeepInfra (current default)

Is the task complex?
  (architectural analysis, multi-file reasoning, planning)
    → qwen-3-235b via Cerebras
    → zai-glm-4.7 via Cerebras (untested, largest)
```

---

## How Models Are Configured

Each ability declares its model in its lib wrapper. The model is a constant at the top of the file.

**DeepInfra** — `functions/src/lib/deepinfra.ts`:
```typescript
const MODEL = "openai/gpt-oss-120b";
```

**Cerebras** — `functions/src/lib/cerebras.ts`:
```typescript
const MODEL = "gpt-oss-120b";
```

To change the model for an ability, change the constant. To use a different provider, import the appropriate lib wrapper.

---

## Secret Management

All API keys live in Google Cloud Secret Manager, accessed via `defineSecret` in Cloud Functions.

| Secret Name | Provider | How to Set |
|---|---|---|
| `DEEPINFRA_API_KEY` | DeepInfra | `firebase functions:secrets:set DEEPINFRA_API_KEY` |
| `CEREBRAS_API_KEY` | Cerebras | `firebase functions:secrets:set CEREBRAS_API_KEY` |
| `GITLAB_TOKEN` | GitLab | Already configured |
| `GITLAB_WEBHOOK_SECRET` | GitLab | Already configured |

---

*Updated Feb 27, 2026. Check provider dashboards for current pricing and model availability.*

title: Module Inventory Crawl
content:
# Module Inventory Crawl

A systematic audit of every project to classify components as reusable or project-specific. This article is the brief — Claude Code reads it and executes.

---

## Objective

Crawl every project in `~/projects/` and produce a module extraction list: what can be pulled into the shared layer, where it currently lives, what it depends on, and what package it should become.

---

## Projects to Crawl

| Project | Description | Expected shared components |
|---|---|---|
| **Launchpad** | Headless Firebase agent, webhooks, LLM pipelines | GitLab API helpers, LLM call patterns, prompt loading, queue format |
| **Raven-os-dev** | Chat-based AI companion, MVC + event-driven | Auth, conversation storage, entity extraction, context retrieval, session management |
| **phoenix** | Workflow/bulletin board system | Bulletin board pattern, task management |
| **phoenix-command** | CLI tools, Kimi search bridge | Search bridge, CLI patterns |
| **skill-command** | Skill management CLI | Skill loading/resolution patterns |
| **desloppify** | Code cleanup tool | Code analysis patterns |
| **fusion-vault** | Data vault/storage | Storage patterns, file management |
| **solo-books** | Book/content management | Content pipeline patterns |
| **task-command** | Task queue CLI | Task queue, may merge into queue-adapter |

---

## What to Look For

For each project, classify every significant component:

### Category: Shared Handler
A unit of work that appears in 2+ projects or could serve any project.

**Identify:**
- LLM call wrappers
- Entity extraction logic
- Context assembly/retrieval
- Document evaluation/correction
- Search/query functions

### Category: Shared Infrastructure
Plumbing that every project needs.

**Identify:**
- Auth/login flows
- Secret/API key management
- Session management
- Database adapters (Firestore, SQLite)
- HTTP client wrappers
- Error handling patterns

### Category: Shared UI Component
Frontend pieces that could be reused across projects.

**Identify:**
- File explorers
- Form builders
- Navigation shells
- Notification/toast systems
- Modal/dialog patterns

### Category: Project-Specific
Only makes sense in this project's context. Leave in place.

### Category: Dead Code
Built once, never used, no clear future use. Archive or delete.

---

## Output Format

For each extractable component, produce:

```markdown
### Component Name

- **Source:** project/path/to/file.ts
- **Category:** Shared Handler | Shared Infrastructure | Shared UI
- **Target package:** @cylon-skin-job/package-name
- **Dependencies:** list of other shared components it needs
- **Complexity:** Low | Medium | High (how much refactoring to extract)
- **Notes:** any caveats, decisions needed, or blockers
```

---

## Execution Instructions

1. Clone or access each project listed above
2. Read the entry point files (`index.ts`, `server.js`, `package.json`) to understand structure
3. Walk the source tree, reading key files (not every line — focus on exports, interfaces, and patterns)
4. For each significant component, classify it using the categories above
5. Produce the extraction list in the output format
6. Flag any components where the classification is ambiguous — mark as "needs decision"
7. Note any duplicated logic found across projects (same thing built twice)

---

## What This Enables

The extraction list becomes the work queue for the shared layer buildout. Each item is a unit of work:

1. Extract component from source project
2. Publish as shared package
3. Replace original with import
4. Verify nothing broke

Items can be prioritized by how many projects would benefit (high reuse = extract first) and how complex the extraction is (low complexity = extract first).

---

## Known Duplications (from prior sessions)

These are already confirmed as shared across projects:

| Component | Found in | Found in |
|---|---|---|
| LLM call wrapper | Launchpad (`deepinfra.ts`) | Raven OS (`backgroundTasks.js`) |
| Conversation-to-JSON | Raven OS (`conversationManager.js`) | Not yet in Launchpad |
| Firebase Auth | Raven OS (`auth.js`) | Not yet in Launchpad |
| Entity extraction | Raven OS (background tasks) | Planned for Launchpad |
| Session timeout | Raven OS (`timeout.js`) | Not yet in Launchpad |
| GitLab API helpers | Launchpad (`gitlab.ts`) | Not yet in Raven OS |

---

- [Shared Layer](Shared-Layer) — what extracted modules become
- [Platform Vision](Platform-Vision) — why this audit matters
- [Handler Pattern](Handler-Pattern) — the interface extracted handlers implement

title: Platform Vision
content:
# Platform Vision

One platform, many interfaces. Everything else is a lens on the same stack.

---

## The Goal

Build a personal automation platform that eliminates duplicated effort across every project, every tool, and every workflow. Not an app — a foundation layer that any project plugs into and immediately inherits scheduling, processing, context retrieval, secret management, and background intelligence.

The end state: say what you want done. The system does it, spawns whatever background work is needed, and surfaces results when they're ready. You never think about plumbing again.

---

## Why This Exists

Every project built so far has reinvented the same pieces:

- **Raven OS** — conversation storage, entity extraction, context retrieval, wiki as knowledge graph, heartbeat for background work
- **Launchpad** — webhook handling, LLM evaluation, wiki as knowledge graph, pulse for background work
- **Code editor workflow** — conversation in, insight extraction (manual), skills/wiki as knowledge graph, no background processing yet
- **Fusion Vault / Ex-JW Librarian** — scraping, enrichment, vector search, job queue with status tracking
- **Phoenix** — bulletin board task queue, heartbeat, specialist agent spawning

The input source differs. The output destination differs. The middle is identical: text goes in, structured understanding comes out, it gets stored in a graph, and downstream things react.

These aren't separate projects. They're separate interfaces to the same platform that hasn't been unified yet.

---

## The Core Insight: One Queue

Everything runs through a single queue. Wiki edges, code reviews, video clips, entity extraction, calendar syncs, document enrichment — all just entries with a timestamp and an action tag.

```
Queue Entry = { fire_after, @action, payload }
```

The queue doesn't care what's in it. The pulse doesn't care what it wakes up. The dispatcher matches `@action` to a handler and calls it. The handler is the only thing that carries domain knowledge.

This means:
- Adding a new capability = writing a handler + registering an action tag
- No changes to the pulse, dispatch, or queue
- No separate instance for video, code, wiki, calendar, or anything else
- The chain is promptless — it's plumbing. Prompts live inside handlers.

---

## The Architecture

```
Pulse (shared service, ticks once/min, broadcasts timestamp)
  ↓
Dispatch (shared package, queries queue, routes by @action)
  ↓
Queue (single spot, fire_after timestamps, self-scheduling elements)
  ↓
Handlers (domain-agnostic units: swap the prompt, swap the domain)
  ↓
Inflow adapters (chat agent, webhook, system event, manual)
  ↓
Interfaces (Raven OS, Launchpad, code editor, dashboard, future projects)
```

Every layer is reusable. Every layer is a module. New projects install the shared layer, wire up their inflow adapter, write their prompts, and they're running.

---

## The Prompt is the Lens

The handler pattern means the entire pipeline is domain-agnostic. The only thing that changes between use cases is the prompt.

| Use case | Prompt | Same pipeline? |
|----------|--------|----------------|
| Wiki edge discovery | "Should these two pages link?" | Yes |
| Doc correction | "Does this markdown have errors?" | Yes |
| Entity extraction | "Who was mentioned in this text?" | Yes |
| Code review | "Are there bugs in this code?" | Yes |
| Video processing | "Clip this file, 0:30-1:45, add title card" | Yes |
| Medical research | "Does this paper cite concepts from this other paper?" | Yes |
| Calendar sync | "What changed since last check?" | Yes |

The prompt is the lens. The stack is the telescope. You build the telescope once.

---

## Self-Scheduling Elements

Individual elements control their own cadence by writing `fire_after` timestamps. The pulse is a dumb clock — it just ticks. Elements decide when they wake up.

- A wiki custodian writes `fire_after = now + 30min` after each run
- A calendar sync writes `fire_after = now + 60min`
- A low-priority enrichment job writes `fire_after = now + 6hours`
- An urgent chat command writes `fire_after = now` (fires on next tick)
- A wiki edit writes `fire_after = now + 11min` (debounce)

All in the same queue, woken by the same pulse, dispatched by the same dispatcher. Zero coordination between them.

---

## Fractal Automation

Processing one queue entry can spawn new ones. This is the core mechanism that makes the system self-sustaining.

A wiki page is created. Issues are filed. The heartbeat evaluates each pair. Edges are written. Writing edges edits pages. Edits trigger re-evaluation. The system converges on its own. No orchestrator coordinates this.

The same applies to code: a commit triggers a review. The review finds issues. Issues spawn fix suggestions. Fixes are applied. Fixes trigger validation. Validation passes or spawns new issues. The chain trickles out.

---

## Launchpad as Proving Ground

Launchpad is where every shared component gets built first. Wiki edge discovery, the issue queue, the pulse, the dispatch — all prototyped here. When something proves stable, it gets extracted into the shared layer (GitLab Package Registry) and becomes available to every other project.

Launchpad stays Launchpad. It just stops carrying its own copies of universal pieces and imports them instead.

---

## Raven OS as Destination

Raven OS is the consumer platform — the personal AI companion that sits on top of all of this. Calendar, email, messages, relationships, spending, to-do lists, wikis, code sessions — all feeding into the same queue, processed by the same handlers, stored in the same graph.

The wiki becomes the user manual for the human-AI relationship. Not static docs — a living knowledge graph that updates itself as context changes.

---

## The Wiki as Communication Layer

The GitLab wiki serves a specific role right now: it's the persistent memory layer that survives across sessions, tools, and agents. Cursor reads it. Claude Code reads it. Future tools will read it.

Any agent, in any session, in any project, can check the wiki to understand:
- What's been built
- Why decisions were made
- What's planned
- What's in progress

The wiki is also the first proof of the handler pattern. Edge discovery runs as a queue-processable unit. The wiki maintains itself.

Eventually, the wiki moves off GitLab and into the owned stack. The pattern stays the same.

---

## The Pulse

A shared service that fires once a minute and broadcasts nothing but a timestamp. Every project subscribes. Elements self-schedule by writing their own `fire_after` into the queue. The pulse checks, the dispatch routes, the handlers execute.

Priority, urgency, scheduling cadence — all collapsed into one field: a timestamp. Set it to now and it fires on the next tick. Set it to tomorrow at 9am and it's a calendar event. The queue doesn't know the difference.

---

## What This Enables

- Code sessions produce structured insights that persist and transfer between tools
- Background agents run code sweeps, validation, and security checks without blocking the user
- New projects inherit the entire shared layer on day one
- Auth, payments, and UI shells are modules — built once, installed everywhere
- The system improves itself: processing creates new work that feeds back into the same queue
- Video production, content pipelines, and any future domain plug in with zero infrastructure changes

---

## The Three Parts

**Raven is the brain.** She provides memory, continuity, and context. She remembers what you worked on before, remembers what tools were used, and remembers where to find the thing you don't know how to find. She knows *what* and *why*.

**The pipeline is the hands.** It executes what Raven can't do herself — code builds, video edits, document processing, security scans, enrichment runs. It knows *how*. Handlers carry the domain expertise. The queue carries the work.

**The wiki is the nervous system.** It connects brain to hands. Raven says "this needs to happen," writes a queue entry, the pipeline executes it. When it's done, the result flows back into Raven's memory. She's smarter for the next conversation. The system learns from its own output.

This is the product. Not an AI assistant. Not a code pipeline. Not a video editor. A nervous system that remembers, plans, and executes — and gets better every time it runs.

---

## Related

- [Shared Layer](Shared-Layer) — the infrastructure modules
- [Handler Pattern](Handler-Pattern) — how work units are structured
- [Build Order](Build-Order) — the sequence in which everything gets built
- [Coding Pipeline](Coding-Pipeline) — the 12-step pipeline preserved in Firestore
- [Extraction Strategy](Extraction-Strategy) — how shared code gets pulled from existing projects
- [Module Inventory Crawl](Module-Inventory-Crawl) — the systematic audit of all projects

---

title: Progressive Disclosure
content:
# Progressive Disclosure

How context flows from always-present orientation to on-demand depth.

---

## The Problem

AI agents drown in context. Load everything at once and the signal disappears in noise. Load nothing and the agent makes decisions without knowing the constraints. The question is not "what should the agent know" — it is "when should it know it."

---

## The Layers

Context is organized into four layers. Each layer is progressively deeper and progressively more expensive to load.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 1: ALWAYS-PRESENT                        │
│  CLAUDE.md / always-apply rules                 │
│                                                 │
│  Loaded: Every conversation, automatically      │
│  Size: ~500 tokens                              │
│  Contains:                                      │
│  · What this project is                         │
│  · Permanent constraints (TypeScript only, etc) │
│  · Pointers to where depth lives                │
│  · How to access the wiki                       │
│                                                 │
│  Rule: If it never changes, it belongs here.    │
│  If it might change, it belongs deeper.         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LAYER 2: SKILLS (on-demand, situational)       │
│  ~/.cursor/skills/ and ~/.claude/skills/         │
│                                                 │
│  Loaded: When the agent recognizes a trigger    │
│  Size: ~500-2000 tokens each                    │
│  Contains:                                      │
│  · Heuristics: when does this matter?           │
│  · Guardrails: what to never do                 │
│  · Enough to steer without going deeper         │
│  · Pointers to wiki articles when depth needed  │
│                                                 │
│  Two subtypes:                                  │
│  · Procedural — self-contained, the skill IS    │
│    the knowledge (async-await, export-safety)   │
│  · Pointing — thin heuristic + wiki pointer     │
│    for volatile or detailed content             │
│                                                 │
│  Rule: Skills are durable and static.           │
│  If it changes when a tool ships an update,     │
│  it belongs in the wiki, not the skill.         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LAYER 3: WIKI (living, self-healing)           │
│  GitLab wiki articles                           │
│                                                 │
│  Loaded: When agent reads via API               │
│  Size: Varies (full articles)                   │
│  Contains:                                      │
│  · Full format specifications                   │
│  · Architecture decisions and rationale         │
│  · Model/provider details                       │
│  · Step-by-step procedures                      │
│  · Comparison tables                            │
│  · Anything that drifts with the system         │
│                                                 │
│  Rule: This is the living truth.                │
│  Skills point here. The wiki self-updates.      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LAYER 4: UPSTREAM DOCS (external truth)        │
│  Official documentation sites                   │
│                                                 │
│  Loaded: When agent fetches a URL               │
│  Contains:                                      │
│  · cursor.com/docs/                             │
│  · code.claude.com/docs/                        │
│  · agentskills.io/specification                 │
│  · Provider API docs                            │
│                                                 │
│  Rule: Wiki articles link here.                 │
│  When upstream changes, update the wiki.        │
│  Never embed upstream detail in skills.         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## How It Works in Practice

1. Agent starts a conversation. CLAUDE.md loads automatically (~500 tokens). Agent knows what the project is, the permanent constraints, and that a wiki exists.

2. Agent encounters a situation — say, picking an LLM model. A skill fires: "Check the wiki for model details." The skill alone might be enough if the agent just needs to know "use DeepInfra for cheap tasks."

3. If the agent needs specifics — rate limits, pricing, model IDs — it reads the wiki article. Full detail, loaded on demand.

4. If the wiki references upstream docs ("see DeepInfra pricing page"), the agent can fetch those too. But usually the wiki has already distilled what matters.

---

## Multi-Wiki Skills

A single skill can point to multiple wiki articles. This is especially useful for pipelines and cross-cutting concerns:

```
Skill: "wiki-evaluation-pipeline"
  → Wiki: Models-and-Providers (which model to use)
  → Wiki: How-It-Works (the evaluation flow)
  → Wiki: Progressive-Disclosure (this article, for context design)
```

The skill becomes a **map** — it tells the agent which articles matter for this kind of work, without loading any of them upfront.

---

## The Brain Wiki

Launchpad serves as the brain center across all projects. Its wiki holds knowledge that applies everywhere — design standards, context architecture, shared conventions.

Individual projects can have their own wikis for project-specific knowledge. A universal orientation skill, present in every project, points agents to both:

- **This wiki** — for cross-project standards and architecture
- **The project wiki** — for project-specific details

---

## What Goes Where

| Content | Layer | Why |
|---|---|---|
| "This is a headless Firebase agent" | CLAUDE.md | Never changes |
| "All code is TypeScript" | CLAUDE.md | Permanent constraint |
| "Check exports before modifying" | Skill | Durable guardrail |
| "Async functions must be awaited" | Skill | Stable pattern |
| "DeepInfra uses model X at rate Y" | Wiki | Changes with providers |
| "Cursor rules use .mdc format" | Wiki | Changes with Cursor releases |
| "How to create a skill" | Wiki | Procedure evolves |
| "Cursor docs on rules" | Upstream | External truth |

---

## Design Principles

1. **No layer loads the one below it automatically.** Each layer is opt-in. The agent decides when to go deeper.

2. **Skills never embed volatile detail.** If it changes when a tool ships an update, put it in the wiki.

3. **The wiki is the single source of truth for anything that drifts.** Don't maintain the same information in both a skill and a wiki article.

4. **CLAUDE.md is orientation, not documentation.** It should be readable in 30 seconds and never exceed ~500 tokens.

5. **A skill that steers correctly without being read deeply is a good skill.** The name and description alone should orient. Loading it should confirm. Reading the wiki should only happen when the agent genuinely needs depth.

## Related

### Build Order
**Stepwise pipeline for self‑maintaining wiki system**

The Build Order outlines the concrete phases needed to realize the self‑healing wiki described in Progressive Disclosure. Its roadmap shows how pulse, dispatch, and edge‑discovery mechanisms bring the layered architecture into operational reality.

### Coding Pipeline
**Example of orchestrated code pipeline**

The coding pipeline serves as a concrete example of how progressive disclosure can structure complex workflows. Its multi‑step orchestration illustrates the need for on‑demand wiki knowledge.

### Module Inventory Crawl
**Systematic audit of reusable project components**

The module inventory crawl supplies the concrete list of shared handlers, infrastructure, and UI pieces that the progressive disclosure framework can expose through skills or wiki layers. It ensures the wiki’s living truth is populated with actual, extracted modules from each project.

### Extraction Strategy
**Extract shared code into reusable packages**

Extraction Strategy outlines a process for moving duplicate logic into shared npm packages. Those packages become the concrete implementations that Progressive Disclosure’s wiki layer can reference, keeping volatile details centralized and DRY.

### Handler Pattern
**Queue‑based handlers that automate wiki edge creation**

title: Shared Layer
content:
# Shared Layer

The infrastructure modules that every project inherits. Built once, installed everywhere.

---

## Overview

The shared layer is a set of services and packages that sit beneath every project. No project implements its own scheduling, queue processing, secret management, or auth. They all point at the same shared modules.

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Interfaces (per project)             │
│   Raven OS  │  Launchpad  │  Dashboard  │  ...   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│              Inflow Adapters                      │
│   Chat agent  │  Webhook  │  System event        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│              Shared Layer                         │
│   Pulse │ Dispatch │ Queue │ Router │ Auth │ UI  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Pulse

A shared Cloud Scheduler service that fires once a minute and broadcasts nothing but a timestamp via Pub/Sub.

**What it does:**
- Ticks once per minute
- Publishes current timestamp to a Pub/Sub topic
- Carries no payload, no routing, no knowledge of subscribers

**What it doesn't do:**
- Know what projects exist
- Know what work is pending
- Make decisions

Each project subscribes to the topic. When the tick arrives, the project's dispatcher wakes up and checks its own queue. Adding a new project = adding a Pub/Sub subscription. No code changes to the pulse itself.

**Cost:** Effectively zero. Cloud Scheduler free tier covers it. Pub/Sub's first 10GB/month is free. A timestamp once a minute is noise.

---

## Dispatch

A shared npm package published to the GitLab Package Registry. Receives the pulse tick, queries the project's queue, and routes work to the correct handler.

```typescript
interface PulseDispatcher {
  onPulse(timestamp: Date): Promise<void>
}
```

Each project provides:
- A **queue adapter** (GitLab issues, Firebase collection, SQLite, whatever)
- **Action handlers** (what to do when `@action` = `edge-eval`, `doc-correction`, etc.)

The dispatcher owns the loop: query queue, filter by `fire_after <= now`, claim, dispatch to handler, mark done. The project owns what the queue looks like and what the handlers do.

**Package:** `@cylon-skin-job/pulse-dispatcher` on GitLab Package Registry.

---

## Queue

The single spot. Every element that needs periodic attention writes a timestamped entry here.

**Current implementation:** GitLab issues with `@fire_after` timestamps, `@action` tags, and `@status` labels.

**Future implementation:** Firebase collection, SQLite table, or any store that supports timestamp queries.

**The universal scheduling primitive:** `fire_after`. Set it to now and it fires on the next pulse. Set it to 30 minutes from now and it waits. Set it to tomorrow at 9am and it's a calendar event. The queue doesn't know the difference.

Elements self-schedule:
- A wiki custodian writes `fire_after = now + 30min` after each run
- A calendar sync writes `fire_after = now + 60min`
- A low-priority enrichment job writes `fire_after = now + 6hours`
- An urgent chat command writes `fire_after = now` (fires on next tick)

All in the same queue, woken by the same pulse, dispatched by the same dispatcher. Zero coordination between them.

---

## Provider Router

A centralized service for API keys, model selection, and endpoint routing.

**The problem:** Every project manages its own secrets via `firebase functions:secrets:set`. Rotating a key means touching every project. Adding a provider means updating every project's config.

**The solution:** One service holds all API keys and provider configs. Projects authenticate to it and request what they need: "I need an LLM," "I need GitLab," "I need Stripe." The service hands back the key and the endpoint.

**What it replaces:**
- Per-project `defineSecret()` calls
- Per-project `.env` files
- Manual secret rotation across N projects
- Hardcoded model names and endpoints

Rotate a key in one place, every project picks it up immediately.

---

## Auth / Login Module

Extracted from Raven OS (where it's already fully working). Firebase Auth with Google sign-in, session management, and user context.

Not copied per project — installed as a module. Same auth flow everywhere, same user identity, same session tokens.

---

## Stripe Module

Payment processing as a shared module. Subscription management, webhook handling, and billing state.

Built once, installed wherever payments are needed. The controller gates which features are available based on subscription tier.

---

## Generic UI Shell

HTML/CSS frame with JavaScript injection points. The shell provides layout, navigation, and responsive structure. Content is injected by each project's controllers.

**Key insight:** With React + TypeScript + Zustand (per the Universal Design Spec), the controller pattern means inputs are identical from app to app. The controller decides what CRUD operations are allowed, which subscriptions are active, and what content appears. The shell stays the same.

Components like the file explorer already exist (built for an earlier project). They don't get rebuilt — they get extracted into the shared layer and installed where needed.

---

## Distribution

All shared modules live in the GitLab Package Registry under the `@cylon-skin-job` scope:

```
@cylon-skin-job/pulse-dispatcher
@cylon-skin-job/queue-adapter
@cylon-skin-job/provider-router
@cylon-skin-job/auth
@cylon-skin-job/ui-shell
```

Any project installs them via npm. Updates propagate by bumping the version.

---

## Related

- [Platform Vision](Platform-Vision) — why the shared layer exists
- [Handler Pattern](Handler-Pattern) — the units of work that run on this infrastructure
- [Extraction Strategy](Extraction-Strategy) — how shared code gets pulled from existing projects
- [Issue Queue](Issue-Queue) — the current queue implementation (GitLab issues)

---

*Updated Feb 27, 2026.*

The Coding Pipeline demonstrates a concrete use‑case of the Shared Layer's pulse, dispatch, and queue modules in orchestrating multi‑step code builds. Its adoption validates the shared infrastructure across complex workflows.

The Shared Layer provides the packages into which the Module Inventory Crawl extracts reusable code. Referencing the crawl process helps teams understand how new modules are identified and integrated into the shared infrastructure.

By feeding edge‑discovery work into the shared infrastructure, onWikiEvent leverages the universal scheduling primitive without needing project‑specific code.

### Platform Vision

The Shared Layer provides the concrete services—Pulse, Dispatch, Queue, Provider Router, Auth, and UI Shell—that embody the Platform Vision. By installing these modules, each project automatically inherits the unified infrastructure described in the vision.

### Models and Providers

The Shared Layer’s Provider Router relies on a definitive list of model providers and their credentials. Linking to the Models and Providers page ensures each project pulls up‑to‑date provider details and secret names.

The "How It Works" page demonstrates a real project that consumes the shared modules such as the provider router, queue dispatcher, and authentication layer. Seeing this implementation helps developers understand how the abstract shared layer is applied in practice.

The onGitLabPush Cloud Function is a concrete implementation that leverages the shared dispatch and queue mechanisms defined in the Shared Layer. Understanding this workflow illustrates how projects can reuse the shared infrastructure for automated document processing.

### Launchpad

Launchpad is one of the consumer projects that installs the shared modules, relying on the Pulse scheduler and Provider Router for LLM access. By using the Shared Layer, Launchpad avoids reinventing auth, queue, and payment handling.

### Development Guide
**Project-level guide leveraging shared modules**

The Shared Layer supplies reusable services such as Pulse, Dispatch, Provider Router, and Auth that the project described in the Development Guide consumes. Following this guide shows how to install and integrate those shared npm packages into a new ability.

### Development Guide
**Example project implementing shared modules**

The Development Guide shows how a concrete project consumes the shared services such as the pulse dispatcher, auth module, and provider router. It demonstrates the real‑world usage that validates the shared layer’s design.

### Executor Contract
**Job schema and execution protocol for Launchpad and Phoenix**

The Shared Layer's Dispatch and Queue modules use the Executor Contract's job schema to claim, execute, and record results from Phoenix. By adhering to this contract, shared services can reliably orchestrate work across projects while maintaining a uniform interface.

### GitHub‑GitLab Workflow
**Two‑repo strategy enabling clean source and AI**

The GitHub‑GitLab workflow illustrates a practical use‑case for the Shared Layer, mirroring code to GitLab where Pulse and Dispatch run. It shows how separating the source of truth from the AI playground aligns with the shared services architecture.

# Update Handler

Design for `action: "update"` in `onWikiEvent`. Not yet built.

---

## The Problem

When a wiki page is edited, its existing `## Related` edges may no longer be accurate. The content changed — the edges should be re-evaluated from scratch.

The naive approach would be to re-evaluate only the existing edges ("are these still valid?"). But that anchors the LLM on prior decisions. A page that gains new content might warrant new edges that weren't there before. A page that changes direction might lose edges that once made sense.

---

1. **Strips the `## Related` section** from the page — removes all existing edge entries

[links]`** — marks the page as pending re-evaluation
3. **Closes any open `status:pending` issues** for this page — cancels the previous queue if still waiting
4. **Files N-1 fresh issues** — identical to the create flow

The heartbeat then evaluates all pairs cold, with no knowledge of what edges existed before. Whatever it decides is the answer.

---

## Why No Prior Edge Context

Passing existing edges to the LLM would bias it:
- It might preserve edges it would otherwise drop ("these were linked before, must be for a reason")
- It might miss new edges because it's focused on validating the old ones
- It introduces a feedback loop: prior output influences future output

By stripping and re-evaluating clean, each run is independent. Edges survive because the content warrants them, not because they were there last time.

---

## The 11-Minute Window as Debounce

If a page is edited twice within 11 minutes:

1. First edit fires → `[links]` inserted, N issues filed with `fire_after` = T+11min
2. Second edit fires → `## Related` stripped (already empty), `[links]` re-inserted, old pending issues closed, N fresh issues filed with new `fire_after` = T2+11min

The heartbeat never sees the first set of issues — they were closed before the timer elapsed. Only the final state gets evaluated. This is the debounce behavior.

---

## Implementation Sketch

```typescript
async function handleUpdate(
  projectId: number,
  slug: string,
  title: string,
  res: Response
): Promise<void> {
  const glToken = gitlabToken.value();

  // 1. Fetch current page
  const page = await getWikiPage(glToken, projectId, slug);

  // 2. Strip ## Related section and any trailing [links]
  const stripped = stripRelatedSection(page.content);

  // 3. Re-insert [links]
  const resetContent = stripped.trimEnd() + "

[links]";
  await updateWikiPage(glToken, projectId, slug, resetContent,
    "docs: reset [links] for edge re-evaluation");

  // 4. Close stale pending issues for this page
  await closeStaleIssues(glToken, projectId, slug);

  // 5. File fresh issues — same as create
  await fileEdgeIssues(glToken, projectId, slug, title);
}
```

`stripRelatedSection` removes everything from `## Related` to the next `---` at the same heading level, or to end of file.

---

## What Needs to Be Built

- `handleUpdate()` in `functions/src/onWikiEvent.ts`
- `stripRelatedSection(content: string): string` — content parser
- `closeStaleIssues(token, projectId, slug)` — query open issues by title prefix `[wiki-edge] {slug} <>`, close them
- Extract `fileEdgeIssues()` as a shared helper called by both `handleCreate` and `handleUpdate`

---

## What Stays the Same

The heartbeat does not change. It processes `@action create` and `@action update` issues identically — it just evaluates the pair and writes edges. The `@action` field exists for audit purposes only.

---

## Related

- [onWikiEvent](onWikiEvent) — the function this handler lives in
- [Issue Queue](Issue-Queue) — the queue both create and update feed into
- [Heartbeat](Heartbeat) — what processes the issues after they're filed
- [Wiki Edge Discovery](Wiki-Edge-Discovery) — overall system concept

---

title: Why We Built It This Way
content:
# Why We Built It This Way

This page exists so that future-you and future-AI understand the reasoning behind every decision. Don't undo these decisions without reading this first.

---

## Decision 1: Kill the bridge architecture

**Problem:** The previous system routed prompts through Kimi CLI on a physical laptop to save money on API tokens. This required:
- A WebSocket relay running on Cloud Run
- A `bridge.js` process running on the laptop
- JSON-RPC 2.0 wire protocol handling
- A pairing token system between phone and laptop
- A React frontend for phone control

None of it worked reliably. The bridge had 5 critical bugs. The relay was a single point of failure. The whole thing existed to avoid a $5/month API bill.

**Decision:** Delete all of it. Pay for API tokens. No laptop. No bridge. No relay. No frontend.

**What this means:** There is no UI. There is no server. There is no local process. Everything runs in Firebase Cloud Functions, triggered by webhooks. This is intentional.

---

## Decision 2: Firebase is the agent

**Problem:** Most AI orchestration tools require you to run something locally. That defeats the purpose of having an always-on agent.

**Decision:** Firebase Cloud Functions are the execution layer. Each function is one "ability." The agent is the sum of its abilities. It lives in the cloud. It's always on. It costs nothing when idle (pay-per-invocation).

**What this means:** When you want the agent to do something new, you write one TypeScript file, export it, and deploy. That's it. No daemon, no supervisor, no container management.

---

## Decision 3: DeepInfra for LLM inference

**Problem:** Need fast, cheap inference. Don't need frontier intelligence for doc evaluation.

**Decision:** DeepInfra `openai/gpt-oss-120b` via their OpenAI-compatible API. Standard OpenAI SDK with custom base URL. $0.039/$0.19 per 1M tokens.

**Why DeepInfra over Cerebras:** Both offer the same model (`gpt-oss-120b`). DeepInfra was chosen because:
- Uses the standard OpenAI SDK — no vendor-specific package needed
- Straightforward pay-as-you-go pricing with no tier-based rate limit concerns
- OpenAI-compatible API means switching providers later is trivial

**Cerebras is still available.** The client wrapper exists at `functions/src/lib/cerebras.ts`. If a future ability needs Cerebras-specific speed (2000-3000 tok/s on their wafer-scale hardware) or models only available there (`qwen-3-235b`, `zai-glm-4.7`), import it.

**Available models on DeepInfra:**
- `openai/gpt-oss-120b` — primary, current default
- `openai/gpt-oss-20b` — cheaper fallback for simple tasks

**Available models on Cerebras:**
- `gpt-oss-120b`, `llama3.1-8b`, `qwen-3-235b`, `zai-glm-4.7`

**What this means:** The model is a one-line change in the lib wrapper. The provider is a one-import change in the ability file. See [Models and Providers](models-and-providers) for full details.

---

## Decision 4: Secrets in GCP Secret Manager, not code

**Problem:** The old repo had committed Firebase service account keys and credential files in git history.

**Decision:** All secrets in Google Cloud Secret Manager. Firebase `defineSecret` injects them at runtime. Nothing in code, nothing in .env files, nothing in git.

**Current secrets:**

| Name | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `DEEPINFRA_API_KEY` | DeepInfra LLM API authentication (active provider) |
| `CEREBRAS_API_KEY` | Cerebras LLM API authentication (secondary provider) |
| `GITLAB_TOKEN` | GitLab API access (read repos, commit changes, update wiki) |
| `GITLAB_WEBHOOK_SECRET` | Verify webhook requests actually come from GitLab |

**What this means:** If you need a new secret for a new ability, run `firebase functions:secrets:set NAME`. Declare it in the function with `defineSecret`. Done.

---

## Decision 5: Dual push to GitHub and GitLab

**Problem:** Code lives in two places. Pushing to one and forgetting the other creates drift.

**Decision:** Git is configured so `git push origin master` hits both GitHub and GitLab simultaneously.

**What this means:** You never push to just one. Both are always in sync. GitLab is the one with the webhook; GitHub is the backup.

---

## Decision 6: Firestore rules deny everything

**Problem:** Old rules were `allow read, write: if true` — completely open to the internet.

**Decision:** Rules set to `allow read, write: if false`. Cloud Functions use the Admin SDK which bypasses rules entirely. No client can read or write Firestore directly.

**What this means:** If you ever build a frontend again, you'll need to add specific rules for it. Right now, the lockdown is intentional.

---

## Decision 7: The agent evaluates its own docs

**Approach:** On push, the agent gets the diff, finds changed `.md` files, and asks the LLM if they're stale or wrong. If the LLM finds issues, it commits corrected versions back to the repo.

**Intention:** Documentation should maintain itself. If you change code, the agent catches doc drift. If you write something with a mistake, the agent corrects it.

**Limitation:** The agent only evaluates `.md` files that changed in the push. It doesn't scan the whole repo for drift. It also can't know about things outside the diff context — if you delete a function but don't touch the doc that references it, the agent won't catch it until the doc itself is modified. This is a future ability to build (periodic full-repo scan).

---

## What's Preserved From Before

Firebase project `launchpad-ai-orchestrator` was kept intact. Firestore still has data from the old system:
- `templates` — pipeline template definitions
- `jobs` — job records
- `runs` — run history
- `ledger` — append-only event log

This data isn't used by any current ability. It's historical. Don't delete it — future abilities may use it.

---

*Every decision above was made on Feb 27, 2026. If you're reading this and thinking about reverting one, read the problem statement first.*

## Related

### Build Order

title: Wiki Edge Discovery
content:
# Wiki Edge Discovery

How wiki pages get cross-linked automatically when created or edited.

---

## What We're Building

A system that maintains a knowledge graph of wiki articles. When a page is created or edited, the system evaluates which other pages should link to it (and vice versa), then writes `## Related` sections with descriptions.

---

## The Flow

```
Wiki create/edit → GitLab webhook → onWikiEvent → ### Build Order
**sequencing of automated pipeline phases**

Wiki Edge Discovery is a core component of the overall Build Order, enabling the system to automatically generate and maintain links as part of the wiki pipeline. It provides the knowledge‑graph foundation that drives later phases such as markdown adjustment and skills auto‑update.

### Handler Pattern
**Generic handlers drive edge discovery pipelines**

The Wiki Edge Discovery system relies on the generic Handler Pattern to process link‑evaluation tasks. By using handlers it can spawn follow‑up jobs, maintain audit trails, and ensure robust, composable automation.

### Shared Layer
**shared infrastructure for scheduling & routing**

Wiki Edge Discovery leverages the Shared Layer’s Pulse scheduler to trigger link‑generation jobs and uses the Dispatch package to process queued edit events. It also taps into the Provider Router for API keys when consulting external knowledge sources.

### Platform Vision
**Unified automation platform with shared queue**

The Platform Vision provides the underlying pulse, queue, and dispatch infrastructure that makes Wiki Edge Discovery possible. By leveraging the shared automation layer, edge discovery runs as a self‑scheduling handler across all projects.

### Update Handler
**processes edit events for page updates**

Wiki Edge Discovery runs when the Update Handler signals that a page has been edited. The handler provides the edit context needed for edge discovery to evaluate and create new links.

### Platform Vision
**foundational automation platform and shared queue**

Wiki Edge Discovery relies on the shared queue, pulse, and dispatcher described in the Platform Vision. The broader architecture provides the reusable infrastructure that powers this automatic linking service.

### Heartbeat
**second stage of the discovery pipeline**

Heartbeat supplies the evaluated issue pairs that Wiki Edge Discovery uses to identify linking opportunities. Its polling of GitLab and LLM evaluation are essential for populating the knowledge graph.

### onWikiEvent
**webhook handler queuing edge discovery work**

The onWikiEvent Cloud Function captures GitLab wiki_page events and enqueues work for the edge discovery pipeline. It initiates the process that supplies the data the Wiki Edge Discovery system needs to evaluate and create cross‑links.

### Issue Queue
**Relies on Issue Queue for task scheduling**

Wiki Edge Discovery consumes the issues created by the Issue Queue to decide which pages to link. The queue provides the persistent, inspectable tasks that drive the discovery process.

### Progressive Disclosure
**Layered context design benefits from auto-linked wiki**

The Wiki Edge Discovery system ensures that the Wiki layer is automatically cross‑linked, making deeper context reachable on demand. Including the Progressive Disclosure article demonstrates how agents can navigate layered information efficiently.

### Models and Providers
**LLM providers powering auto‑link generation**

The Wiki Edge Discovery system uses LLMs to analyse article content and propose links. The Models and Providers page lists the available inference engines, informing which model the edge‑creation logic should invoke.

### How It Works
**Technical mechanism behind automatic cross‑linking**

The detailed implementation described in "How It Works" explains how the webhook, LLM, and Firestore components identify and create reciprocal links. Understanding this stack is essential for maintaining and extending the edge discovery system.

### Launchpad
**Automated linking for Launchpad's documentation**

Launchpad runs a self‑maintaining wiki of its own architecture and processes, providing a rich set of pages that evolve continuously. Applying Wiki Edge Discovery here would automatically create meaningful cross‑links as new pages are added or edited.

### Development Guide
**Example project page benefiting from auto linking**

The Development Guide is a typical wiki page that can be automatically linked by the Wiki Edge Discovery system, showcasing its utility for technical documentation. Linking to this guide improves navigation for developers working on new abilities and infrastructure.

### Update Handler
**processes page update events**

Wiki Edge Discovery relies on the Update Handler to know when a page has been edited and trigger link evaluation. Without the handler, edge discovery would have no trigger to run.

### GitHub-GitLab Workflow
**AI-driven code sync and automated documentation linking**

The GitHub‑GitLab workflow illustrates a practical scenario where automated agents suggest cross‑links, demonstrating the need for edge discovery. It benefits from the Wiki Edge Discovery system to keep the knowledge graph current and reliable.

[links] placeholder + issues filed
                                                          ↓
                                              (11 min later)
                                                          ↓
Heartbeat polls → picks ripe issues → LLM evaluates pair → writes edges or rejects → closes issue
```

---

## Components

### onWikiEvent (deployed)

Firebase HTTP function. Receives GitLab `wiki_page` webhook.

**On create:**
1. Append `[links]` to the new page (placeholder + validation flag)
2. File one GitLab issue per existing wiki page
3. Each issue has `fire_after` = now + 11 minutes

**On edit/delete:** Not yet implemented.

### Heartbeat (future)

Self-draining poller. Every 60 seconds, queries for ripe `wiki-edge` issues (`@status pending` + `@fire_after < now`). Claims them, runs LLM evaluation per pair, writes `## Related` entries or rejects, closes issues. Stops when queue is empty.

---

## Edge Format

Each edge in a `## Related` section:

```markdown
### Models and Providers
**How model choice affects evaluation quality**

The model used for diff evaluation directly affects correction quality.
Different providers have different context windows and response formats.
```

- `###` header = linked article title
- Bold line = 5-10 word relevance hook
- Two sentences = why this article matters from this page's perspective

Edges are symmetric: if A links to B, B links to A. Descriptions differ (each describes the other from its own viewpoint).

---

## Issue Format

Issues use `@` directives for machine parsing:

```
@fire_after 2026-02-27T19:11:00.000Z
@status pending
@action create
@new_page New-Page-Slug
@existing_page Existing-Page-Slug

Edge discovery: evaluate whether these two wiki pages should be cross-linked.
```

**Status lifecycle:** `pending` → `claimed` (heartbeat processing) → `handled` (closed)

---

## The [links] Placeholder

Literal text `[links]` appended to new pages. Serves two purposes:

1. **Placeholder** — marks where `## Related` entries will go
2. **Validation flag** — any page still containing `[links]` has unresolved edges (backlog issue #2: validation script to scan for these)

---

## The 11-Minute Timer

Every issue gets `fire_after` = now + 11 minutes. Gives breathing room: if someone creates a page and immediately edits it, duplicate events bump the timer. No evaluation runs until 11 minutes of silence.

---

## Related

- [How It Works](How-It-Works) — overall architecture
- [Models and Providers](Models-and-Providers) — LLM used for edge evaluation (Cerebras)
- [Development Guide](Development-Guide) — how to add abilities

---

*Updated Feb 27, 2026.*

3. Lists all existing wiki pages
4. Files one GitLab issue per existing page (N-1 issues)
5. Logs the result to Firestore (`agent_actions` collection)

---

## Webhook Verification

Every request is verified against `GITLAB_WEBHOOK_SECRET` via the `X-Gitlab-Token` header. Requests that fail verification receive `401`. Non-POST requests receive `405`.

The secret is stored in Google Cloud Secret Manager and accessed via `defineSecret`.

---

## The Create Flow

```
POST /onWikiEvent
  ↓ verify X-Gitlab-Token
  ↓ check object_kind === "wiki_page"
  ↓ check action === "create"
  ↓ getWikiPage(slug)          → fetch current content
  ↓ updateWikiPage(slug)       → append [links] to content
  ↓ listAllWikiPages()         → get all other pages
  ↓ createIssue() × N-1       → one per existing page
  ↓ Firestore log              → agent_actions collection
  ↓ 200 JSON response
```

Issue filing is best-effort: if one issue fails, the function logs the error and continues with the remaining pages. A partial failure is logged to Firestore.

---

## Issue Format Filed

```
Title:  [wiki-edge] new-slug <> existing-slug
Labels: wiki-edge, status:pending

@fire_after 2026-02-27T19:11:00.000Z
@status pending
@action create
@new_page new-slug
@existing_page existing-slug

Edge discovery: evaluate whether these two wiki pages should be cross-linked.
```

See [Issue Queue](Issue-Queue) for the full format reference.

---

## Firestore Log

Every invocation logs to `agent_actions`:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `type` | `"onWikiEvent"` |
| `subType` | `"wiki_page_create"` |
| `projectId` | GitLab project ID |
| `pageSlug` | Slug of the created page |
| `pageTitle` | Title of the created page |
| `wikiUpdated` | Whether `[links]` was successfully appended |
| `issuesCreated` | Count of issues filed |
| `issueDetails` | Array of `{slug, iid, webUrl}` |
| `timestamp` | Server timestamp |

On error: same fields plus `error` message, `wikiUpdated` and `issuesCreated` reflect state at time of failure.

---

## Stubbed Actions

| Action | Current behavior |
|---|---|
| `create` | Fully implemented |
| `update` | Returns 200, logs "not yet implemented" |
| `delete` | Returns 200, logs "not yet implemented" |

See [Update Handler](Update-Handler) for the design of the update path.

---

- [Issue Queue](Issue-Queue) — the coordination layer this function feeds into
- [Wiki Edge Discovery](Wiki-Edge-Discovery) — overall system concept
